title: Simplest DIY Bed Frame (All Mattress Sizes)
summary: A 2x6 box frame with 2x2 cleats and screwed-down 1x4 slats — no headboard, no footboard, just the minimum structure a mattress needs, in five real sizes.
description:
This is the bed frame stripped to its studs: a 2x6 perimeter box, 2x2 cleats to carry the slats, and a center 2x4 rail so a queen or king doesn't sag in the middle. There's no headboard or footboard here at all — pair it with one you already own, lean it against a wall, or add a simple panel of your own later, since the frame itself doesn't depend on either.

The plan gives five complete, genuinely distinct cut lists — twin through California king — not one size scaled by guesswork, so build to the numbers for your actual mattress rather than interpolating between two sizes. King-size beds need the additional center leg supports in the last step; smaller sizes can skip them. The corner bracing is optional but adds real rigidity for very little extra material, and it's a good place to spend an extra 20 minutes if the bed will see rough use.

materials.name: Pine, 2x6, 8 ft
materials.note: Sized for twin — add boards per the size-specific shopping list for larger mattresses.

materials.name: Pine, 1x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Slats — scrap lumber works as long as the thickness is consistent.

cutList.note: Twin length shown — see the size-specific note for other mattress sizes.
Twin width shown; 57"/63"/79"/75" for full/queen/king/cal-king respectively.
Half slats can be used if working from scrap.

steps.title: Cut the frame parts
steps.body: Cut for the size you are actually building; the numbers in the cut list are the twin. For a twin, cut two side rails 75" and two top/bottom rails 41" from the 2x6, two cleats 75" from the 2x2, ten slats 38" from the 1x4, and — if you want the optional bracing — four corner braces 8" from 2x6 with both ends at 45°. The top/bottom rail length is your mattress width plus 3", which is why the note reads 57", 63", 79" and 75" for full, queen, king and cal-king: those two rails run the full outside width of the box and the side rails sit between them. Three 8 ft 2x6s cover all the 2x6 work — a 75" side rail plus two 8" braces on each of two boards, and both 41" rails on the third. Each 8 ft 2x2 gives one 75" cleat, and each 8 ft 1x4 gives two 38" slats, so the five listed 1x4s yield exactly ten.

steps.title: Attach the cleats to the side rails
steps.body: Lay each 75" side rail inside-face up and run a 75" 2x2 cleat along it, flush with the rail's bottom edge and flush at both ends. Drive 2-1/2" screws through the cleat into the rail every 10" to 12", staggering them so the 2x2 does not split along one grain line. With the cleat at the bottom, the 3/4" slats end up 2-1/4" above the bottom of the 5-1/2" rail and the 2x6 still stands about 3-1/4" proud of the slat surface, which is what keeps the mattress from sliding off the sides. Do both rails identically, and be sure the cleats end up on the faces that will be inside the box.

steps.title: Assemble the box
steps.body: Stand the two 41" top/bottom rails on edge and set the 75" side rails between them, cleats facing inward and all top edges flush. Drive three 2-1/2" screws through the face of each end rail into the end grain of each side rail, pre-drilling so the 2x6 does not split. That gives a box 41" x 78" outside with a 38" x 75" opening — exactly a twin mattress — so measure both diagonals and rack the frame until they match before the last screws go home.

steps.title: Add the center support
steps.body: For full size and up, run a center rail down the length of the box between the two end rails — the same clear span as the side rails, 75" on a twin-length frame — and set it so its top face is flush with the tops of the 2x2 cleats, because the slats have to bear on the cleats and the center rail at the same time. Screw it to the end rails with 2-1/2" screws driven through the outside faces. Be aware that the materials list and cut list contain no 2x4 line for this part, so add one board to your shopping for anything larger than a twin.

steps.title: Add optional corner bracing
steps.body: Cut the four 8" corner braces so the long edge measures 8" and the two 45° ends run opposite ways, which lets each brace sit flat across an inside corner with both ends bearing on the rail faces. Tuck each brace down against the cleats so it cannot foul the slats, then drive two 2-1/2" screws through the side rail and two through the end rail into it. They are optional, but on a 78"-long box they are what stops the frame racking when someone sits down on a corner.

steps.title: Lay and attach the slats
steps.body: Lay the ten 38" slats across the cleats with one slat tight against each end rail and the remaining eight spread evenly between. Ten 3-1/2" slats use 35" of the 75" span, leaving 40" of gap across nine spaces — about 4-7/16" between slats. Drive one 1-1/2" to 2" screw through each slat end into the cleat below; one screw per end is enough and it lets a slat be lifted out later without splitting it. Do not glue them.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Turn the frame over and mount a leg at each corner, set in far enough that the mounting plate lands fully on the 1-1/2" thickness of both rails rather than overhanging the outside face. Use the screws that came with the legs — a 2-1/2" screw driven through a 1-1/2" rail comes out the other side. Turn it back over and check it does not rock before the mattress goes on.

steps.title: Add center leg support for king sizes
steps.body: A king or cal-king frame is 79" or 75" across, and at that span the center rail needs its own path to the floor rather than hanging off the end rails. Add one leg under the middle of the center rail, or two spaced evenly along it, using the same leg hardware as the corners. Note that the cut list does not actually contain the center leg parts this step refers to, so budget an extra leg set for a king-size build.

images.alt: A minimal 2x6 box bed frame with slats and legs, no headboard or footboard
